$650 at Best Buy $552 at Amazon

CAT might be best known for making construction equipment, but they make phones, too. And the CAT S62 Pro is built to handle the same job sites as the company's other products. This phone has been drop-tested up to six feet and exceeds MIL-STD-810H protection to guard it against vibration, shock, and extreme temperatures from -4 °F to 392 °F. Thanks to its IP68 and IP69K rating, it can survive complete immersion. Plus, it can even be wiped clean using antibacterial wipes and sprays or even bleach.

And while many rugged phones come with cameras, the CAT S62 Pro has a FLIR thermal imaging camera. You can combine this thermal imaging with pictures from the main 12MP camera to create hybrid images to pinpoint leaks, hot spots, and other problems on the job. The thermal camera can also add linear detail from a scene to let you see even more of what's going on and get a fuller picture of your job site.

The CAT S62 Pro has a 4,000mAh battery, which is not quite as long-lasting as other rugged phones. And it doesn't support 5G, though that isn't too unusual in this product category. If you're looking for a premium rugged phone that takes excellent thermal images, the CAT S62 Pro has everything you need.

Source: Ulefone
UleFone Power Armor 16 Pro
Best value

Plenty of power at a lower price

The UleFone Power Armor 16 Pro is an affordable, rugged phone with an incredibly loud and powerful speaker that's ideal for busy, crowded job sites. On top of that, you get an extra-bright flashlight along with a 9,600mAh battery that can last up to 52 hours of call time.

Pros
  • Powerful speaker
  • High-capacity battery
  • Android 12
Cons
  • Not Verizon compatible
  • Main camera is small
$190 at Amazon

If you need an affordable option, the UleFone Power Armor 16 Pro is a great choice. It can survive dust, splashes, immersion up to 5 feet for 30 minutes, and more because of its IP68 and IP69K ratings and has been tested for MIL-STD-810G. And if you work on loud job sites, the 122 dB speaker is sure to come in handy. That way, you'll never miss a call, or you can just use it to play some tunes while you work. For comparison, many alarms come in at around 108dB, and this phone gets louder than that.

For night jobs or dim situations, you can use the 126-lumen flashlight to cut through the gloom. And the Power Armor 16 Pro can work in temperatures ranging from -4 °F to 131 °F. Furthermore, the 9,600mAh battery can last up to 52 hours of talk time, so you won't have to stop and charge very often.

The UleFone Power Armor 16 Pro has a small 16MP main camera, however, and it only supports 4G. But at its price point, you'd be hard-pressed to find a model that's more useful and durable.

Source: Samsung
Samsung Galaxy XCover6 Pro
Ecosystem integration

A rugged phone from a classic brand

The Samsung Galaxy XCover6 Pro is a rugged phone from the same ecosystem as many well-known flagship models. But it still offers durability, including an IP68 rating, shock- and vibration-proofing, and can survive immersion up to 5 feet for 30 minutes. Plus, it supports 5G connectivity, and you can take out the battery.

Pros
  • Compatible with the larger Samsung ecosystem
  • Removable battery
  • 5G compatible
Cons
  • Small-to-average battery
  • Selfie cam is small
$760 at Amazon

If you have already bought into the larger Samsung ecosystem or want to get started, the Samsung Galaxy XCover6 Pro is a solid option. This phone works with the brand's other products, and I'd say Samsung makes some excellent Android phones in general. This model has a rubber bumper to protect the screen from drops, while the touchscreen itself is still useable with gloves on. And the IP68 rating ensures it's dust-proof and waterproof. Furthermore, it comes with virtual tools, including a gyroscope, barometer, accelerometer, and more, so you'll always have the right gadget for the job.

With 6GB of RAM and 128GB of storage space (that you can expand to 1TB), this phone will handle almost any app easily. And for something truly rare in the smartphone world these days, the 4,050mAh battery is replaceable, so even though it's not quite as long-lasting as other rugged phones' batteries, you can swap it out in a snap once it wears out.

There's also a 50MP rear camera, which takes clear shots, but the 13MP front camera is rather small, even for a selfie cam. Still, it works well for conference calls with colleagues. Plus, this rugged phone supports 5G, which is still not found on many models. For ecosystem-integrated workplaces, the Samsung Galaxy XCover6 Pro is a great fit.

Source: Amazon
Nokia XR20 5G
Stylish pick

Good-looking with rugged performance

The Nokia XR20 is a good-looking phone that's still rugged, thanks to its IP68 rating. It has been built to MIL-STD-810H standards, too. In fact, you can even rinse it clean if it gets dirty. Plus, it's 5G compatible, and Nokia promises four years of security updates.

Pros
  • 5G compatible
  • Sleek, stylish design
Cons
  • No charger in the box
  • Battery is just average
$349 at Amazon

For the fashion-conscious, there is the Nokia XR20. This phone is sleek and stylish, but it still has an IP68 rating and has been built to MIL-STD-810H standards, so it survives falls, bumps, and more. If it does happen to get dirty, you can clean it with soap and water. Plus, the Gorilla Glass Victus touchscreen will still work even if you have wet hands. Nokia also promises to provide security updates for four years, so your data will stay safe, too.

You get a 48MP camera to capture everything you need while on the job. The 13MP front camera is rather small, but it's fine for selfies or conference calls. And OZO spatial audio recording technology helps cut down on wind noise, so both your visuals and sound will be crisp and clear.

The Nokia XR20 has a 4,630mAh battery, which is alright, but you don't get a charger in the box. Many manufacturers do that nowadays, though. And you get 5G compatibility, which is still not found on every rugged phone. Overall, Nokia XR20 does a good job of blending good looks with reliable performance.

Source: Amazon
Ulefone Armor 9
Best for thermal imaging

A rugged phone with a FLIR camera

$420 $470 Save $50

The Ulefone Armor 9 has a thermal imaging camera that is useful for many types of jobs, and its camera setup is quite powerful overall.

Pros
  • Thermal imaging camera
  • Good battery life
Cons
  • Android 10
  • Only supports 4G
$420 at Amazon

With its ability to capture detailed thermal images and take shots at night using infrared night vision, the Ulefone Armor 9 is handy on many kinds of job sites. Its FLIR camera works between 14 °F to 752 °F and lets you scan scenes to help locate items, inspect equipment, detect leaks, and even find people, while its night mode takes clear shots in low light. If you wish, you can attach an endoscope to this phone (sold separately) to further increase your imaging capabilities.

In addition to its thermal imaging and night vision capabilities, this phone boasts a 64MP main camera that takes clear shots. And with 8GB of RAM and 1238GB of storage, slowdowns and hiccups will not be a concern. It can even survive immersion up to five feet for 30 minutes.

You do not get 5G support with this phone, and it tops out at Android 10 preinstalled, but that is not unexpected for rugged phones. You still get a camera that's great for imaging and super tough to withstand harsh job sites.

Lenovo ThinkPhone by Motorola
Security-focused option

Keep your data secure on a tough phone

The Motorola ThinkPhone is a security-focused phone for business users with an IP68 rating, and it has been MIL-STD-81H tested to ensure its durability. It integrates well with the ThinkPad ecosystem for easy syncing between your phone and PC, too.

Pros
  • Four years of security updates with security-focused features
  • Great PC integration
  • Rugged build
  • 5G compatible
Cons
  • Camera is just average
  • May require a case for especially tough job sites
$700 at Motorola

The Motorola ThinkPhone is a security-focused phone with a tough build. It features an IP68 rating and has been MIL-STD-810H tested, so it can withstand some bumps and drops. A Gorilla Glass VIctus screen protects it from scratches, while the aluminum frame offers extra durability. Motorola promises four years of security updates, and you can create a folder on your phone that is protected by a pin with a dummy icon. That way, sensitive files will stay out of sight and be safe.

This phone also integrates tightly with the ThinkPad ecosystem. You can swap data between your ThinkPad PC and the Motorola Phone. Furthermore, it's even possible to stream apps or mirror your phone's screen on your computer. It is not strictly necessary to have a ThinkPad to do this, either. Any PC running Windows 10 or 11 that has the Ready For app installed will work.

The ThinkPhone supports 5G, which is also handy. Note that while it's sturdily built, I'd say you may want to invest in a rugged phone case for it because this phone may be slightly more vulnerable than some of the other options on this list. However, it will keep your sensitive files safe, and that's good peace of mind to have on some job sites.

Source: Verizon
Kyocera DuraSport
Verizon compatible

Verizon 5G comes to a rugged phone at last

The Kyocera DuraSport 5G UW is a rugged phone that works with Verizon, which is still somewhat of a rare find. It survives immersion in water up to 6.5 feet deep for 30 minutes, plus you can sanitize it if it gets dirty and keep on working.

Pros
  • Verizon 5G compatible
  • Compact and portable
  • Bright, easy-to-read display
Cons
  • Some performance hiccups
  • Low-light photos are lackluster
$580 at Verizon

It can be tricky to find a phone that is rugged, 5G-compatible, and works on Verizon's network. Thankfully, there is the Kyocera DuraSport 5G UW. It is built just as tough as other phones on this list, with an IP68 rating, and has been MIL-STD-810H tested. It can even survive immersion in water up to 6.5 feet deep for 30 minutes and be sanitized if it gets dirty. That makes it ideal for busy, messy job sites. The Gorilla Glass 6 screen is reinforced at the corners for extra durability, and the phone's seams and joins are waterproofed. Still, despite being so tough, it's not too bulky or awkward.

You get a super bright 6.1-inch display that works great in both sunlight and indoors. And taps still register with gloves, which is nice during jobs in the middle of winter. Plus, it comes with handy apps for a compass, barometer, weather, and more.

The 48MP camera does well during the day or in brightly lit rooms, but low-light conditions tend to produce muddier images. And you may run into some performance hiccups on its Android 11 operating system, though nothing is too sluggish to be usable. If you need a Verizon-compatible rugged phone, the Kyocera DuraSport 5G UW fits the bill nicely.

Source: Amazon
Ulefone Power Armor 13
Best for measuring

A rugged phone with an infrared distance meter

The Ulefone Power Armor 13 has an infrared distance measurer, which is sure to be useful on job sites. It also boasts a 48MP camera and a large 6.81-inch screen. Plus it comes with handy apps like a plumb bob, protractor, compass, and more.

Pros
  • Infrared distance measuring
  • Long battery life
  • Big screen
Cons
  • Heavy
  • Bulky
  • Only 4G and Android 12
$170 at Amazon

Many rugged phones carry a standard set of features, and the Ulefone Power Armor 13 is no different with its IP68 and IP69K ratings. But this phone also has an infrared distance measurer, which means you can get the length, area, volume, and even the Pythagorean distance between all sorts of things on a job site. If you wish, you can also add an optional sold-separately endoscope as well. Plus, apps like a protractor and compass come built-in.

A 48MP main camera takes clear snapshots, while the 8MP wide-angle and 2MP macro lenses let you take broad panoramas and extreme closeups. The 13200mAh battery lasts for nearly five days of regular use or 17 hours of continuous talk time. And with 8GB of RAM and 256GB of storage space, you won't experience hiccups or slowdowns.

Note that the Ulefone Power Armor 13 only comes with Android 12 and 4G support, which is common for rugged devices. It also tips the scale at 17.35 ounces (492 g), which is over a pound. But its infrared measuring capabilities are unbeatable if you need them.

The best rugged smartphones in 2023: The final takeaway

My overall pick for the best rugged smartphone is the Doogee V MAX. It is waterproof, dust-proof, drop-resistant, and even works in extreme temperatures. Plus, it comes with a great set of cameras and 5G support. If you want something a bit more premium, consider the CAT S62 Pro, which comes from the same brand that makes construction equipment. It boasts an excellent thermal camera and a solid build. For the value-minded, I'd recommend the Ulefone Power Armor 16 Pro, which has an incredibly loud speaker and a high-capacity battery, all in a durable package that can survive harsh job sites.

Source: Doogee
Doogee V MAX
Best overall
$464 $580 Save $116

The Doogee V MAX offers a rugged IP68/IP69K rating and has been MIL-STD-810H tested to withstand the harshest of conditions. This phone gives you plenty of features that are useful on every job site, including a night-vision camera, long battery life, and even 5G support.

$464 at Amazon

item.title: Best 1080p GPU in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best 1080p GPU in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Full HD was all the rave back in the early 2010s. TVs, laptops, and monitors were all leaving the 720p resolution behind, in favor of 1080p. It's hard to fathom just how quickly display tech advanced as we're now seeing graphics cards capable of displaying images to screens with four times as many pixels (2160p or 4K). Still, 1080p is a common resolution today as it's less demanding on PC hardware, allowing those who primarily play esports and value high frame rates above visual fidelity to enjoy excellent experiences. Here are the best 1080p GPUs for gaming.

  • Source: NVIDIA
    NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Ti
    Best 1080p GPU
    $350 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    Asus Dual GeForce RTX 4060
    Runner-up best 1080p GPU
    $300 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    AMD Radeon RX 7600
    Best value 1080p GPU
    $265 at Amazon
  • Source: Intel
    Intel Arc A770 Limited Edition
    Best Intel 1080p GPU
    $405 at Amazon
  • Source: 51RISC
    NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 Super
    Best budget 1080p GPU
    $227 at Amazon
  • AMD Radeon RX 6600
    Best last-gen 1080p GPU
    $210 at Amazon
  • Source: AMD
    AMD Radeon RX 7800 XT
    Best performance 1080p GPU
    $520 at Newegg
  • Image: Nvidia
    Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070
    Best ray tracing 1080p GPU
    $600 at Amazon

item.title: Best Apple Watch models in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best Apple Watch models in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you have bought one of the great iPhones, there's no better smartwatch than the Apple Watch. Other manufacturers don't offer the same level of deep integration that the Cupertino firm provides between the Apple Watch and the iPhone. With so many versions having been released so far, however, it's easy to wonder if you should opt for the newest one and why the newer ones are the best options. That’ll require some explaining.

  • Apple Watch Series 9
    Best for most users
    $390 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch Ultra 2
    Best for extreme athletes
    $799 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch SE 2
    Best budget watch
    $249 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch Series 8
    Alternative option
    $310 at Amazon

Our top picks for Apple Watch models in 2023

Apple Watch Series 9
Best for most users

The ideal Apple Watch for most

Apple's Watch Series 9 packs in a new, more powerful processor, a brighter display capable of up to 2,000 nits peak brightness, Apple's U2 chip that enables precision-finding of your iPhone as well as better HomePod integration, and a whole slew of features and tech you'd expect.

Pros
  • New S9 chip for better performance
  • Better display with up to 2000nits brightness
  • Second-generation Ultra Wideband chip
Cons
  • Looks exactly the same as Series 8
  • Series 8 watch can now be found for cheaper
$390 at Amazon $400 at Best Buy $400 at Apple

The Apple Watch Series 9 is only a minor improvement over the outgoing Series 8 model, but it's currently the best option to pick if you're in the market to buy a new smartwatch to pair with your iPhone. This updated model is powered by the company's new S9 chip, which has 60% more transistors than the S8 chip for better performance and power efficiency. It also has a more advanced neural engine that enables on-device processing for Siri.

Notably, the Apple Watch Series 9 also has a new ultra-wideband chip that allows it to do precision tracking to locate things like the iPhone, AirTags, and more. Another highlight of this particular watch is the support for Apple's new "Double Tap" feature. This feature, in case you don't know, allows you to quickly tap your thumb and index finger twice to trigger an action on the watch while wearing it. This feature is exclusive to the Apple Watch models with watchOS 10 and the new S9 chip, meaning you can't use it on the older watches.

Some other highlights of the new Apple Watch Series 9 include a brighter display, which now peaks at 2000 nits outdoors, and a new Pink color option that goes very well with the Pink iPhone 15 model. The rest of the features are identical to the Series 8, so it comes in the same 41mm and 45mm sizes and keeps the IP6X certification for dust resistance and swim-proof WR50 water resistance. You also get an always-on display on the Series 9 watch, and it supports heart-rate tracking, ECG and blood oxygen monitoring, and more.

The Apple Watch Series 8 is also a great option to consider if you don't want all the bells and whistles, but you may have a hard time finding them in stock.

Apple Watch Ultra 2
Best for extreme athletes

A great companion on any adventure

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 is the latest top-tier smartwatch from Apple, powered by the S9 chipset and featuring a super-bright display.

Pros
  • Updated S9 for better performance
  • Better display with up to 3000 nits brightness
  • Second-generation Ultra Wideband chip
Cons
  • Same design as the previous model
  • Not a huge improvement over its predecessor
$799 at Amazon $799 at Apple $799 at Best Buy

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 is the updated version of the original rugged watch that came out last year. This new watch looks exactly the same as the outgoing model, but Apple has made some noteworthy improvements to the internals, making it a good option to consider. Just like the Apple Watch Series 9, the Apple Watch Ultra 2 is also powered by the new S9 chip, which is expected to have better performance and power efficiency. We're yet to put this chip through its paces, but Apple is promising a solid performance improvement.

The Apple Ultra 2 also supports on-device processing for Siri, and you also get precision tracking for compatible products. The highlight of this watch, however, is its display that supports up to 3000 nits brightness, making it the brightest display Apple has ever designed. It'll ship with watchOS 10 out of the box, so you can expect to see all the new features, including the new and intuitive Double Tap gesture. The rest of the features are the same as the Apple Watch Ultra that came out last year, so you're looking at the same titanium case with a customizable Action button on the side.

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 can also track all your activities and vital metrics like blood oxygen levels, heart rate, and sleep stages, among other things. We'll update this space with a more measured take once we've had the chance to test it. But in the meantime, you can stop by our Apple Watch Ultra review to get a better understanding of the kind of experience you'd have with this watch. It only comes in a single color and a 49mm size, but you get to pick between three different official bands, each of which caters to a particular kind of extreme sport.

Apple Watch SE 2
Best budget watch

A perfect intro into the Apple Watch ecosystem

Apple's budget smartwatch might be lacking some features like an always-on display, the fastest charging, and ECG, but it's still a well-designed Apple product and a capable smartwatch for those looking for a cool piece of wearable tech that won't cost anywhere near as much as a Watch Ultra 2.

Pros
  • Will meet most needs, with some limitations
  • Same processor as the Series 8 Watch
  • One of the most affordable options
Cons
  • No Always-on display
  • Not as durable
  • No ECG, blood oxygen monitoring, body temperature measurement
$249 at Amazon $249 at Best Buy

The $249 Apple Watch SE 2 is the best model available for those who don't want to spend a fortune on a wearable. Released along with the Series 8 and Ultra, this model packs most of the watchOS features you know and love. While it lacks some tempting features like AOD, it offers the same Apple Watch experience — for the most part, at least.

For starters, all the fundamental sensors and offerings are included. So you get to track your activity, use Apple Pay, text/call people, and more. You even get the S8 processor included in the Series 8. The biggest differences between the SE 2 and Series 8 include the screen size options, Always-On display support, and other health-related features.

The Apple Watch SE 2 is available in two sizes: 40mm and 44mm. It has thicker bezels and lacks Always-On display support, so the screen only lights up when you trigger it. It has a nylon composite back case versus the stronger ceramic back case of the Series 9 model. The triggers include a wrist tilt, a screen tap, and more. Additionally, this watch misses out on the body temperature monitor, ECG, and blood oxygen monitor. You'll have to decide if this is worth the savings.

So if you have worries or health conditions revolving around a certain missing sensor, you might want to refrain from buying the Apple Watch SE. But if you don't care about the thicker bezels, smaller screen, lack of AOD, and the aforementioned health sensors' absence, then this watch is the best model to go for. Even if you're not on a limited budget, if the listed perks aren't of interest to you, you can safely buy the SE 2 and enjoy most of what this wearable category has to offer.

Apple Watch Series 8
Alternative option

Worth it if you find one for a steal

$310 $400 Save $90

The Apple Watch Series 8 is the 2022 smartwatch from Apple. It misses out on some new features, but it remains a solid option to consider for those who don't mind giving up some features in favor of a relatively cheaper price.

Pros
  • Has many of the same features as the Series 9 model
  • Can save a ton if you find one for a killer deal
  • Updated to watchOS 10
Cons
  • Older, outdated model
  • Often found for the same price as the Series 8
$399 at Best Buy $310 at Amazon $310 at Walmart

The Apple Watch Series 8 is no longer available to purchase officially, but you should be able to find one for a relatively cheaper price. It's not as powerful or feature-packed as the Apple Watch Series 9, but it supports a lot of the new features and looks exactly the same as the newer model. Apple has also updated this watch to the watchOS 10 software, meaning you're not missing out on much.

You can already find this particular watch for as low as $310, which is an excellent price for what it brings to the table. It still costs a bit more than the Apple Watch SE 2, but it also brings a better display and tracks more health metrics. It also looks a lot more premium, so it's worth considering over the SE 2 if your budget allows for it.

Best Apple Watch models in 2023: The bottom line

Hopefully, this has helped you figure out the best watch model for you. As noted, we find the Apple Watch Series 9 to be the best option overall, offering good value for money and all the latest and greatest features. You aren't sacrificing features, and you'll have the newest option on your wrist. Those who are more invested in activities like diving, hiking, and other extreme sports should consider buying the Apple Watch Ultra 2 instead. Not only is it more durable, but it's also a lot more capable, offering a bigger and better display, better battery life, etc.

Apple Watch Series 9
Best overall

Apple's Watch Series 9 packs in a new, more powerful processor, a brighter display capable of up to 2,000 nits peak brightness, Apple's U2 chip that enables precision-finding of your iPhone as well as better HomePod integration, and a whole slew of features and tech you'd expect.

$390 at Amazon $400 at Best Buy $400 at Apple

If, on the other hand, this is your first Apple Watch and you simply want to get a feel for how it works, then don't discount the Apple Watch SE 2. It's a solid option that will meet most peoples' needs. Lastly, since you’re buying one of these smartwatches, don’t forget to check out our picks for the best Apple Watch bands, in which we've highlighted some great picks.

  • Same design as the previous model
  • Not a huge improvement over its predecessor
$799 at Amazon $799 at Apple $799 at Best Buy

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 is the updated version of the original rugged watch that came out last year. This new watch looks exactly the same as the outgoing model, but Apple has made some noteworthy improvements to the internals, making it a good option to consider. Just like the Apple Watch Series 9, the Apple Watch Ultra 2 is also powered by the new S9 chip, which is expected to have better performance and power efficiency. We're yet to put this chip through its paces, but Apple is promising a solid performance improvement.

The Apple Ultra 2 also supports on-device processing for Siri, and you also get precision tracking for compatible products. The highlight of this watch, however, is its display that supports up to 3000 nits brightness, making it the brightest display Apple has ever designed. It'll ship with watchOS 10 out of the box, so you can expect to see all the new features, including the new and intuitive Double Tap gesture. The rest of the features are the same as the Apple Watch Ultra that came out last year, so you're looking at the same titanium case with a customizable Action button on the side.

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 can also track all your activities and vital metrics like blood oxygen levels, heart rate, and sleep stages, among other things. We'll update this space with a more measured take once we've had the chance to test it. But in the meantime, you can stop by our Apple Watch Ultra review to get a better understanding of the kind of experience you'd have with this watch. It only comes in a single color and a 49mm size, but you get to pick between three different official bands, each of which caters to a particular kind of extreme sport.

Apple Watch SE 2
Best budget watch

A perfect intro into the Apple Watch ecosystem

Apple's budget smartwatch might be lacking some features like an always-on display, the fastest charging, and ECG, but it's still a well-designed Apple product and a capable smartwatch for those looking for a cool piece of wearable tech that won't cost anywhere near as much as a Watch Ultra 2.

Pros
  • Will meet most needs, with some limitations
  • Same processor as the Series 8 Watch
  • One of the most affordable options
Cons
  • No Always-on display
  • Not as durable
  • No ECG, blood oxygen monitoring, body temperature measurement
$249 at Amazon $249 at Best Buy

The $249 Apple Watch SE 2 is the best model available for those who don't want to spend a fortune on a wearable. Released along with the Series 8 and Ultra, this model packs most of the watchOS features you know and love. While it lacks some tempting features like AOD, it offers the same Apple Watch experience — for the most part, at least.

For starters, all the fundamental sensors and offerings are included. So you get to track your activity, use Apple Pay, text/call people, and more. You even get the S8 processor included in the Series 8. The biggest differences between the SE 2 and Series 8 include the screen size options, Always-On display support, and other health-related features.

The Apple Watch SE 2 is available in two sizes: 40mm and 44mm. It has thicker bezels and lacks Always-On display support, so the screen only lights up when you trigger it. It has a nylon composite back case versus the stronger ceramic back case of the Series 9 model. The triggers include a wrist tilt, a screen tap, and more. Additionally, this watch misses out on the body temperature monitor, ECG, and blood oxygen monitor. You'll have to decide if this is worth the savings.

So if you have worries or health conditions revolving around a certain missing sensor, you might want to refrain from buying the Apple Watch SE. But if you don't care about the thicker bezels, smaller screen, lack of AOD, and the aforementioned health sensors' absence, then this watch is the best model to go for. Even if you're not on a limited budget, if the listed perks aren't of interest to you, you can safely buy the SE 2 and enjoy most of what this wearable category has to offer.

Apple Watch Series 8
Alternative option

Worth it if you find one for a steal

$310 $400 Save $90

The Apple Watch Series 8 is the 2022 smartwatch from Apple. It misses out on some new features, but it remains a solid option to consider for those who don't mind giving up some features in favor of a relatively cheaper price.

Pros
  • Has many of the same features as the Series 9 model
  • Can save a ton if you find one for a killer deal
  • Updated to watchOS 10
Cons
  • Older, outdated model
  • Often found for the same price as the Series 8
$399 at Best Buy $310 at Amazon $310 at Walmart

The Apple Watch Series 8 is no longer available to purchase officially, but you should be able to find one for a relatively cheaper price. It's not as powerful or feature-packed as the Apple Watch Series 9, but it supports a lot of the new features and looks exactly the same as the newer model. Apple has also updated this watch to the watchOS 10 software, meaning you're not missing out on much.

You can already find this particular watch for as low as $310, which is an excellent price for what it brings to the table. It still costs a bit more than the Apple Watch SE 2, but it also brings a better display and tracks more health metrics. It also looks a lot more premium, so it's worth considering over the SE 2 if your budget allows for it.

Best Apple Watch models in 2023: The bottom line

Hopefully, this has helped you figure out the best watch model for you. As noted, we find the Apple Watch Series 9 to be the best option overall, offering good value for money and all the latest and greatest features. You aren't sacrificing features, and you'll have the newest option on your wrist. Those who are more invested in activities like diving, hiking, and other extreme sports should consider buying the Apple Watch Ultra 2 instead. Not only is it more durable, but it's also a lot more capable, offering a bigger and better display, better battery life, etc.

Apple Watch Series 9
Best overall

Apple's Watch Series 9 packs in a new, more powerful processor, a brighter display capable of up to 2,000 nits peak brightness, Apple's U2 chip that enables precision-finding of your iPhone as well as better HomePod integration, and a whole slew of features and tech you'd expect.

$390 at Amazon $400 at Best Buy $400 at Apple

If, on the other hand, this is your first Apple Watch and you simply want to get a feel for how it works, then don't discount the Apple Watch SE 2. It's a solid option that will meet most peoples' needs. Lastly, since you’re buying one of these smartwatches, don’t forget to check out our picks for the best Apple Watch bands, in which we've highlighted some great picks.
item.content:encoded:
If you have bought one of the great iPhones, there's no better smartwatch than the Apple Watch. Other manufacturers don't offer the same level of deep integration that the Cupertino firm provides between the Apple Watch and the iPhone. With so many versions having been released so far, however, it's easy to wonder if you should opt for the newest one and why the newer ones are the best options. That’ll require some explaining.

  • Apple Watch Series 9
    Best for most users
    $390 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch Ultra 2
    Best for extreme athletes
    $799 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch SE 2
    Best budget watch
    $249 at Amazon
  • Apple Watch Series 8
    Alternative option
    $310 at Amazon

Our top picks for Apple Watch models in 2023

Apple Watch Series 9
Best for most users

The ideal Apple Watch for most

Apple's Watch Series 9 packs in a new, more powerful processor, a brighter display capable of up to 2,000 nits peak brightness, Apple's U2 chip that enables precision-finding of your iPhone as well as better HomePod integration, and a whole slew of features and tech you'd expect.

Pros
  • New S9 chip for better performance
  • Better display with up to 2000nits brightness
  • Second-generation Ultra Wideband chip
Cons
  • Looks exactly the same as Series 8
  • Series 8 watch can now be found for cheaper
$390 at Amazon $400 at Best Buy $400 at Apple

The Apple Watch Series 9 is only a minor improvement over the outgoing Series 8 model, but it's currently the best option to pick if you're in the market to buy a new smartwatch to pair with your iPhone. This updated model is powered by the company's new S9 chip, which has 60% more transistors than the S8 chip for better performance and power efficiency. It also has a more advanced neural engine that enables on-device processing for Siri.

Notably, the Apple Watch Series 9 also has a new ultra-wideband chip that allows it to do precision tracking to locate things like the iPhone, AirTags, and more. Another highlight of this particular watch is the support for Apple's new "Double Tap" feature. This feature, in case you don't know, allows you to quickly tap your thumb and index finger twice to trigger an action on the watch while wearing it. This feature is exclusive to the Apple Watch models with watchOS 10 and the new S9 chip, meaning you can't use it on the older watches.

Some other highlights of the new Apple Watch Series 9 include a brighter display, which now peaks at 2000 nits outdoors, and a new Pink color option that goes very well with the Pink iPhone 15 model. The rest of the features are identical to the Series 8, so it comes in the same 41mm and 45mm sizes and keeps the IP6X certification for dust resistance and swim-proof WR50 water resistance. You also get an always-on display on the Series 9 watch, and it supports heart-rate tracking, ECG and blood oxygen monitoring, and more.

The Apple Watch Series 8 is also a great option to consider if you don't want all the bells and whistles, but you may have a hard time finding them in stock.

Apple Watch Ultra 2
Best for extreme athletes

A great companion on any adventure

The Apple Watch Ultra 2 is the latest top-tier smartwatch from Apple, powered by the S9 chipset and featuring a super-bright display.

Pros
  • Updated S9 for better performance
  • Better display with up to 3000 nits brightness
  • Second-generation Ultra Wideband chip
Cons
  • Same design as the previous model
  • Not a huge improvement over its predecessor
$799 at Amazon $799 at Apple $799 at Best Buy

We'll update this space with a more measured take once we've had the chance to test it. But in the meantime, you can stop by our Apple Watch Ultra review to get a better understanding of the kind of experience you'd have with this watch. It only comes in a single color and a 49mm size, but you get to pick between three different official bands, each of which caters to a particular kind of extreme sport.

item.title: Free vs paid VPN: Which is best for you?
item.itunes:title: Free vs paid VPN: Which is best for you?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Whether you want to protect your privacy online or just want to cover your security bases while using public Wi-Fi, virtual private networks (VPN) put another layer between your device and the internet. The best VPNs work by routing your internet connection through a VPN server before accessing the rest of the internet, putting a secure tunnel between your device and the servers from which you’re pulling data. VPNs are a good way to give yourself some peace of mind online.

Free VPNs, however, are a bit trickier to trust because, at the end of the day, somebody has to pay for those servers. Many free VPNs are offered as more of a trial for a full service with restrictions that make them impossible to use all the time. Even so, these free VPNs can be excellent in a pinch for downloading a few emails or sending messages when you’re away from your home or work network.

Free VPNs have data and speed limits

The most significant restriction with free VPNs is data. Free VPNs often give you just enough data to browse the web or access email without worry, but if you try to download larger files, you’ll find that you run out of data rather quickly. Streaming video and attending online meetings can also add up rapidly.

One of the best free VPN services, Hotspot Shield, comes with 500MB per day on the free plan. That’s less than an hour of Disney+ with quality set to Save Data. If you plan accordingly, however, and stick to light usage, these free VPNs can be an excellent way of protecting yourself online as long as they’re from a reputable provider.

Proton VPN, on the other hand, doesn’t have a data cap, but speeds can be lower than paid accounts, and streaming isn’t supported. The paid version comes with faster VPN speeds and more servers in more countries. Using an unlimited paid VPN means you can leave it connected all the time without giving up too much speed or worrying about running out of data.

Paid VPNs have more servers

A VPN is nothing without its servers, and much of the time, the best servers are reserved for paying customers. Proton VPN, for example, has over 100 servers in three countries for free users but 2,900+ servers in 65 countries for paid ones. The capacity of these paid servers can be higher, but mostly, there are simply more of them to go around. If you look at Proton VPN’s server page, you’ll also see that the free servers are often very close to full capacity, which means that speeds can suffer. Proton VPN’s paid servers also support P2P file transfer, such as BitTorrent and Tor.

Whether you’re trying to view sites locked to certain regions or save money on merchandise that’s sold cheaper in another country, a VPN with plenty of servers gives you the best chance for success. Some VPN services even specify servers for streaming with extra upkeep to keep them from getting locked out.

Protect your privacy

While many free VPNs enjoy the same level of privacy as their paid counterparts, some companies may take advantage of you. VPNs may not be as safe as they seem, and at the end of the day, your network traffic is passing through the VPN, so you want to be sure the company isn’t holding onto records any longer than they absolutely have to and has a proven record of respecting users’ privacy.

Your browsing information is valuable and can be used to learn a great many things about you in addition to your shopping habits. A free VPN needs to make money somehow to keep operating, so you want to be sure that it's not doing that by selling your data. Advertisers hate wasting their money, and if they can already identify an intent to purchase based on your browsing habits, their ads can be much more effective.

A free VPN needs to make money somehow to keep operating, so you want to be sure that it's not doing that by selling your data.

You also can't know with full confidence whether your stored data is being held securely. If your data isn't properly secured, a bad actor might also be able to use it to aid in a phishing scam, such as offering a fake warranty for a product you've likely purchased. While many of us are pretty savvy about tech scams, making one mistake and sharing key information can lead to weeks of headaches.

Reputable VPN providers will tell users exactly how long they keep logs and what type of information is stored. Typically, the best paid VPN providers don't keep any logs at all beyond what's needed to sign you into their software, helping keep your usage private. If you’re considering a VPN, make sure you know what type of data it’s keeping and how long the provider holds onto it.

Paid VPN extras

Most free VPNs will severely limit the number of devices you can have connected through a single account to just one. If you want to get more devices online at a time, a paid VPN will come with many more connected devices, so you can have your phone, tablet, computer, and more protected by the same VPN. You can even get a router with VPN support to protect even more devices without needing to install profiles on each one.

Besides the basics, many paid VPNs have switched to a more full-service format with malware protection and ad-blocking. Surfshark, for example, offers an ad blocker and cookie pop-up blocker with its basic package. The cookie pop-up browser automatically declines cookie requests on websites, so you don’t accidentally accept anything you don’t mean to.

Of course, you can toggle ad-blocking off if you don’t want it or if it’s causing issues visiting a website.

You shouldn’t use a VPN for everything

VPNs sound like a perfect solution from the ads, but they can also disrupt your internet experience in some ways. First and foremost is speed. Even with the latest protocols, you’re very likely giving up some of your internet speed when connecting to a VPN.

You’re also adding another hop to your connection on its way to the destination, which can hurt ping times. Ping is the time it takes for data to be transmitted between your device and the internet. A low ping is essential to responsive online gaming, and while a high-quality VPN adds only a few milliseconds to your connection, it can still impact your game. A VPN can be a reliable way of connecting to servers in another region, but for most people, it should be left off for gaming.

Which VPN should you pick?

When it comes to picking the right VPN, it really comes down to what you need it for. If you’re just grabbing a few emails on public Wi-Fi, a trusted free VPN should do the trick. A free VPN is also fine for general browsing but may fall flat when it comes to streaming music or video. Free VPNs may also struggle to keep up with a video meeting if the server is close to capacity, which can lead to interruption.

Hotspot Shield is one of our favorite free VPNs because it offers a decent chunk of data renewed daily. The 2Mbps connection won't be great for streaming, and neither will the 500MB of data, but for shooting off a few emails in an airport or using financial apps on an unknown public Wi-Fi connection, Hotspot Shield is a good choice.

Hotspot Shield

Hotspot Shield is free and easy to use with a solid 500MB of data per day with up to 2Mbps. While limited to a single connection for free, this VPN has enough data and speed for securely browsing and emailing from a public connection.

See at Hotspot Shield

If you are using a VPN for work, it’s always a good idea to check with your company to see if it already uses a VPN. Many companies and organizations, like colleges, will have VPNs to their network for workers or students who travel. You may also already pay for one with an antivirus package or even Google One.

Finally, a paid VPN makes sense for someone who wants to download large files using BitTorrent, secure multiple devices, and stream without worrying about usage. Paid VPNs support many more servers than free VPNs, so you can find a server with a relatively low load or in a location that works well for your needs to keep performance high. If you want to stream video from another country, a paid VPN with streaming-optimized servers will give you the best overall experience.

One of our favorite paid VPNs is Mullvad, which has plenty of servers to find a strong connection in a wide range of countries. If you're hesitant about paying for your VPN with a card, you can also pay for Mullvad with cryptocurrency, bank wire, or even cash if you mail it in. You don't even need to provide an email address and phone number to Mullvad with everything linked to your generated account number.

Mullvad

Mullvad is an affordable VPN with over 650 servers in 43 countries. Mullvad accepts a wide range of payment methods including credit card, cryptocurrency, bank wire, and even cash.

$5+/mo at Mullvad

The truth is, most of us don’t need to use a VPN all the time, and features like two-factor authentication paired with a little common sense are all the security many people need. Still, a VPN is a very convenient backup plan when you’re using public Wi-Fi that tracks your usage, or a connection that you aren’t quite sure is secure.

If you're hesitant about paying for your VPN with a card, you can also pay for Mullvad with cryptocurrency, bank wire, or even cash if you mail it in. You don't even need to provide an email address and phone number to Mullvad with everything linked to your generated account number.

Mullvad

Mullvad is an affordable VPN with over 650 servers in 43 countries. Mullvad accepts a wide range of payment methods including credit card, cryptocurrency, bank wire, and even cash.

$5+/mo at Mullvad

The truth is, most of us don’t need to use a VPN all the time, and features like two-factor authentication paired with a little common sense are all the security many people need. Still, a VPN is a very convenient backup plan when you’re using public Wi-Fi that tracks your usage, or a connection that you aren’t quite sure is secure.
item.content:encoded:
Whether you want to protect your privacy online or just want to cover your security bases while using public Wi-Fi, virtual private networks (VPN) put another layer between your device and the internet. The best VPNs work by routing your internet connection through a VPN server before accessing the rest of the internet, putting a secure tunnel between your device and the servers from which you’re pulling data. VPNs are a good way to give yourself some peace of mind online.

Free VPNs, however, are a bit trickier to trust because, at the end of the day, somebody has to pay for those servers. Many free VPNs are offered as more of a trial for a full service with restrictions that make them impossible to use all the time. Even so, these free VPNs can be excellent in a pinch for downloading a few emails or sending messages when you’re away from your home or work network.

Free VPNs have data and speed limits

The most significant restriction with free VPNs is data. Free VPNs often give you just enough data to browse the web or access email without worry, but if you try to download larger files, you’ll find that you run out of data rather quickly. Streaming video and attending online meetings can also add up rapidly.

One of the best free VPN services, Hotspot Shield, comes with 500MB per day on the free plan. That’s less than an hour of Disney+ with quality set to Save Data. If you plan accordingly, however, and stick to light usage, these free VPNs can be an excellent way of protecting yourself online as long as they’re from a reputable provider.

Proton VPN, on the other hand, doesn’t have a data cap, but speeds can be lower than paid accounts, and streaming isn’t supported. The paid version comes with faster VPN speeds and more servers in more countries. Using an unlimited paid VPN means you can leave it connected all the time without giving up too much speed or worrying about running out of data.

Paid VPNs have more servers

A VPN is nothing without its servers, and much of the time, the best servers are reserved for paying customers. Proton VPN, for example, has over 100 servers in three countries for free users but 2,900+ servers in 65 countries for paid ones. The capacity of these paid servers can be higher, but mostly, there are simply more of them to go around. If you look at Proton VPN’s server page, you’ll also see that the free servers are often very close to full capacity, which means that speeds can suffer. Proton VPN’s paid servers also support P2P file transfer, such as BitTorrent and Tor.

Whether you’re trying to view sites locked to certain regions or save money on merchandise that’s sold cheaper in another country, a VPN with plenty of servers gives you the best chance for success. Some VPN services even specify servers for streaming with extra upkeep to keep them from getting locked out.

Protect your privacy

While many free VPNs enjoy the same level of privacy as their paid counterparts, some companies may take advantage of you. VPNs may not be as safe as they seem, and at the end of the day, your network traffic is passing through the VPN, so you want to be sure the company isn’t holding onto records any longer than they absolutely have to and has a proven record of respecting users’ privacy.

Your browsing information is valuable and can be used to learn a great many things about you in addition to your shopping habits. A free VPN needs to make money somehow to keep operating, so you want to be sure that it's not doing that by selling your data. Advertisers hate wasting their money, and if they can already identify an intent to purchase based on your browsing habits, their ads can be much more effective.

A free VPN needs to make money somehow to keep operating, so you want to be sure that it's not doing that by selling your data.

You also can't know with full confidence whether your stored data is being held securely. If your data isn't properly secured, a bad actor might also be able to use it to aid in a phishing scam, such as offering a fake warranty for a product you've likely purchased. While many of us are pretty savvy about tech scams, making one mistake and sharing key information can lead to weeks of headaches.

Reputable VPN providers will tell users exactly how long they keep logs and what type of information is stored. Typically, the best paid VPN providers don't keep any logs at all beyond what's needed to sign you into their software, helping keep your usage private. If you’re considering a VPN, make sure you know what type of data it’s keeping and how long the provider holds onto it.

Paid VPN extras

Most free VPNs will severely limit the number of devices you can have connected through a single account to just one. If you want to get more devices online at a time, a paid VPN will come with many more connected devices, so you can have your phone, tablet, computer, and more protected by the same VPN. You can even get a router with VPN support to protect even more devices without needing to install profiles on each one.

Besides the basics, many paid VPNs have switched to a more full-service format with malware protection and ad-blocking. Surfshark, for example, offers an ad blocker and cookie pop-up blocker with its basic package. The cookie pop-up browser automatically declines cookie requests on websites, so you don’t accidentally accept anything you don’t mean to.

Of course, you can toggle ad-blocking off if you don’t want it or if it’s causing issues visiting a website.

You shouldn’t use a VPN for everything

VPNs sound like a perfect solution from the ads, but they can also disrupt your internet experience in some ways. First and foremost is speed. Even with the latest protocols, you’re very likely giving up some of your internet speed when connecting to a VPN.

You’re also adding another hop to your connection on its way to the destination, which can hurt ping times. Ping is the time it takes for data to be transmitted between your device and the internet. A low ping is essential to responsive online gaming, and while a high-quality VPN adds only a few milliseconds to your connection, it can still impact your game. A VPN can be a reliable way of connecting to servers in another region, but for most people, it should be left off for gaming.

Which VPN should you pick?

When it comes to picking the right VPN, it really comes down to what you need it for. If you’re just grabbing a few emails on public Wi-Fi, a trusted free VPN should do the trick. A free VPN is also fine for general browsing but may fall flat when it comes to streaming music or video. Free VPNs may also struggle to keep up with a video meeting if the server is close to capacity, which can lead to interruption.

Hotspot Shield is one of our favorite free VPNs because it offers a decent chunk of data renewed daily. The 2Mbps connection won't be great for streaming, and neither will the 500MB of data, but for shooting off a few emails in an airport or using financial apps on an unknown public Wi-Fi connection, Hotspot Shield is a good choice.

Hotspot Shield

Hotspot Shield is free and easy to use with a solid 500MB of data per day with up to 2Mbps. While limited to a single connection for free, this VPN has enough data and speed for securely browsing and emailing from a public connection.

See at Hotspot Shield

If you are using a VPN for work, it’s always a good idea to check with your company to see if it already uses a VPN. Many companies and organizations, like colleges, will have VPNs to their network for workers or students who travel. You may also already pay for one with an antivirus package or even Google One.

Finally, a paid VPN makes sense for someone who wants to download large files using BitTorrent, secure multiple devices, and stream without worrying about usage. Paid VPNs support many more servers than free VPNs, so you can find a server with a relatively low load or in a location that works well for your needs to keep performance high. If you want to stream video from another country, a paid VPN with streaming-optimized servers will give you the best overall experience.

One of our favorite paid VPNs is Mullvad, which has plenty of servers to find a strong connection in a wide range of countries. If you're hesitant about paying for your VPN with a card, you can also pay for Mullvad with cryptocurrency, bank wire, or even cash if you mail it in. You don't even need to provide an email address and phone number to Mullvad with everything linked to your generated account number.

Mullvad

Mullvad is an affordable VPN with over 650 servers in 43 countries. Mullvad accepts a wide range of payment methods including credit card, cryptocurrency, bank wire, and even cash.

$5+/mo at Mullvad

The truth is, most of us don’t need to use a VPN all the time, and features like two-factor authentication paired with a little common sense are all the security many people need. Still, a VPN is a very convenient backup plan when you’re using public Wi-Fi that tracks your usage, or a connection that you aren’t quite sure is secure.

item.title: Best VPNs for Canada in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best VPNs for Canada in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
A virtual private network (VPN) is an important weapon to have in your online privacy arsenal. Not only does it encrypt your internet traffic, but it also lets you get around geo-restrictions that block web content based on region.

If you’re Canadian, you may be looking for a VPN to protect your privacy and unblock international streaming libraries. Canada also has a degree of internet censorship and ISP throttling. A good VPN hides your internet activity, allowing full access to sites and services your ISP might otherwise interfere with. With that in mind, here are our picks for the best VPNs for Canada in 2023.

All the dollar prices mentioned below are in U.S. dollars, not Canadian dollars.

  • ExpressVPN
    Best overall
    $12.95/mo at ExpressVPN
  • NordVPN
    Best for privacy
    $12.99/mo at NordVPN
  • ProtonVPN
    Best variety
    $12/mo at Proton VPN
  • CyberGhost
    Best streaming performance
    $12.99/mo at CyberGhost
  • TunnelBear
    Best for ISP blocking
    $9.99/mo at TunnelBear
  • Mullvad
    Best value
    $5+/mo at Mullvad
  • Surfshark
    Best for Android
    $15.45/mo at Surfshark

Our top VPNs for Canada in 2023

ExpressVPN
Best overall

Easily access Canadian and international content

With four locations in Canada and thousands spanning the rest of the world, ExpressVPN has the servers, speed, and security to take the top spot.

Pros
  • Four Canadian servers
  • Strong leak prevention
  • Speeds up to 10Gbps
Cons
  • Pricey monthly plan on its own
$12.95/mo at ExpressVPN

ExpressVPN ranks high in many of our best VPN categories, so it'll, of course, excel for Canadians. Whether you’re in another country or traveling outside Canada, it offers four server locations that provide a Canadian IP address: two in Toronto, one in Vancouver, and another in Montreal. This is perfect for unblocking geo-restricted streaming platforms and other sites usually only reserved for those located in Canada.

For Canadians at home, simply connect to the default location. Then, you’ll benefit from 256-bit AES encryption, protecting your real-time internet activity from your ISP, Wi-Fi snoopers, and web trackers. You never have to worry about your real location being exposed because it consistently passes IP, DNS, WebRTC, and IPv6 leak tests. If the VPN connection does drop, the automatic kill switch halts all traffic until you’re reconnected. In total, you get more than 3,000 servers in 94 different countries and 160 cities, which gives you access to all major streaming services and geo-restricted content worldwide.

With a stable 1-10Gbps network, ExpressVPN can easily handle 4K streaming, online gaming, and torrenting. There are also no bandwidth or data usage limits, and you can connect eight devices via a single account. For occasions when you only want to encrypt certain activity, the split tunneling feature lets you choose the specific apps to route through the VPN. Its easy-to-use apps are available on all your devices, including Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, Fire TV, Chromebook, or browser extension.

Choose from a $12.95 per month plan or commit to six months upfront for $9.99 or 12 months for $8.32. For additional privacy, ExpressVPN boasts a no-logs policy and is based in the British Virgin Islands, which is outside the data retention laws of the mainland U.K.

NordVPN
Best for privacy

Stop Canadian ISPs blocking content

NordVPN's special obfuscation technology makes it impossible for Canadian or any other ISPs to block content or throttle speeds. Choose from over 5,000 individual servers in 60 countries.

Pros
  • Hides all activity as regular web traffic
  • Lots of additional privacy measures
  • Billing data secured in Panama
Cons
  • Six device limit
  • Pricey monthly plan
$12.99/mo at NordVPN

All leading VPNs provide 256-bit encryption, but NordVPN has several unique options that make it even more difficult to track or throttle internet activity. Firstly, its obfuscated servers mask all traffic as regular web activity. If your ISP slows down streaming, gaming, or torrenting, choose an obfuscated server to get things running smoothly again. These are available in Canada, the U.S., the EU, and other regions.

Secondly, Double VPN makes a regular VPN connection and then routes it through a second VPN server for double the privacy. The Onion Over VPN feature sends a VPN connection through the Tor network. Both will impact speed and are useful for bypassing banned websites in Canada, strict censorship while traveling overseas, or accessing the dark web. Other options include a kill switch and split tunneling. It also has safe browsing and an ad blocker.

If you’re outside of Canada or just want the VPN for privacy, it has three fast Canadian servers in Montreal, Toronto, and Vancouver. If you’re inside Canada, you can pick from 5,000 servers in 60 countries, granting access to virtually any geo-restricted content in these other countries. For additional privacy, your account is secured in Panama, outside the data surveillance alliances.

You can use NordVPN on six devices across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. It’s $12.99 for a monthly plan. A year upfront costs $59.88 ($4.99 per month), while two years is $102.33 ($3.79 per month).

ProtonVPN
Best variety

More than 100 optimized Canadian servers

From streaming to torrenting, ProtonVPN has all the servers you need for securely accessing the internet inside and outside of Canada. Try it for free from three locations.

Pros
  • Streaming and torrenting servers in Canada
  • Genuine free-forever plan
  • Based in Switzerland for privacy
Cons
  • Unintuitive split tunneling
  • No free Canadian servers
  • Could be faster
$12/mo at Proton VPN

ProtonVPN has more than 100 Canadian servers, many of which are optimized for torrenting and streaming. It recommends Prime Video, DAZN, Netflix, and Disney+, but they still work well for any type of content thanks to a 1Gbps network. Other VPNs are faster, but a single user can still stream in 4K.

For content outside of Canada, you can choose from servers in 69 countries. It’s also one of the few top-tier providers that offers a free-forever VPN plan with unlimited bandwidth, though this restricts you to the Netherlands, Japan, or the U.S.

All the expected VPN features are here, such as a kill switch and split tunneling. However, it’s needlessly difficult to exclude websites because it requires the IP address rather than the URL. As well as maintaining a no-logs policy, the company is based in Switzerland, which puts it outside the common legal jurisdictions for obtaining user account info. Furthermore, it has the Tor Over VPN option, which gives absolute anonymity, even when traveling to countries with heavy internet restrictions like China.

The ProtonVPN app is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android. Plans start from €11.49 or approximately $12.40 per month.

CyberGhost
Best streaming performance

Canadian streaming servers up to 10Gbps

Unblock CBC, Netflix Canada, and DAZN with CyberGhost's optimized streaming servers. Test the performance yourself via a free one-day desktop trial.

Pros
  • 9,000 servers in 91 countries
  • 1Gbps-10Gbps network speed
  • 24-hour free desktop trial
Cons
  • No split tunneling on iOS
  • No free trial on mobile
  • Pricey monthly plan
$12.99/mo at CyberGhost

Like ProtonVPN, the Romania-based CyberGhost has Canadian servers dedicated to streaming. These include CBC, Netflix Canada, and DAZN. While there are only three of these within Canada, it gets the top streaming pick because its speeds are more consistent, ranging between 1Gbps and 10Gbps.

Overall, you get an impressive 9,000 servers in 91 countries, with special options for streaming, gaming, and torrenting. For an extra fee, you can also buy a dedicated IP address in Canada or other countries. This prevents sites from flagging your internet activity as suspicious due to shared IPs with other users or constant IP changes.

CyberGhost has flawless split tunneling, which supports apps and URLs. However, this is conspicuously absent from iOS. You will find it on all its other apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. Up to seven devices can connect at the same time from one account.

Before committing to a premium plan, macOS and Windows users benefit from a full one-day free trial. Then it’s $12.99 per month, $41.94 for six months upfront ($6.99 per month), or $56.94 for two years ($2.19 per month).

TunnelBear
Best for ISP blocking

Bypass Canadian internet censorship

After campaigning for Canadian web users, TunnelBear's GhostBear servers prevent ISPs from throttling speeds and banning content. Try the VPN for free with 2GB of data.

Pros
  • Obfuscated servers
  • Affordable monthly plan
  • No device limits
Cons
  • Fewer countries than most
  • No split tunneling on iOS
$9.99/mo at TunnelBear

For strict ISPs that block access to content and throttle download speeds when streaming or torrenting, TunnelBear is an excellent solution in Canada. It actively campaigns against website blocking and has sent letters to the Canadian government. In practice, the GhostBear feature solves these problems by obfuscating traffic to make it seem like regular web traffic.

It now has 5,000 individual servers that span 47 countries. This includes Canada and countries on every continent. Unlike most VPNs, it also has zero device, connection, or bandwidth limits. This is ideal for users with a lot of devices, multiple users in a household, or even the workplace.

It also has all the key VPN features you’d expect, though they have their own bear branding. VigilantBear is its kill switch, and SplitBear is for split tunneling apps and websites. The latter is not available on the iOS version, but Windows, macOS, and Android users get the full works.

Genuine free trials and plans are rare, so it’s nice to see a no-commitment 2GB plan. Most users will burn through that quickly, but the data is the only limit. All features and server locations are available for free. When you’ve tested the VPN, a monthly plan is a reasonable $9.99, while it’s $59.88 for a year ($4.99 per month) or $120 for three years ($3.33 per month).

Mullvad
Best value

27 servers in Canada for less than $6

Mullvad's fast 1-10Gbps VPN network is available for a cheap monthly fee and has 27 locations to choose from in Canada. It's so private you don't even need to provide your name.

Pros
  • Purchase anonymously
  • Less than $6/mo
  • Up to 10Gbps server network
Cons
  • Five device limit
  • Fewer countries than most
$5+/mo at Mullvad

Sweden-based Mullvad VPN continues to offer unmatched pricing for top-tier features. Instead of the usual $10-$13 for a monthly plan, Mullvad is just 5 euros, or approximately $5.40. That’s one simple plan with no long-term commitments. On the flip side, there are no long-term discounts, but even then, it’s still competitive with some of the providers out there.

You don’t even have to give your name, email, or phone number when joining Mullvad. It generates a unique account number, which is all you need to sign in. The account can be purchased using cryptocurrency, too. It doesn’t get more anonymous than that.

VPN features include a kill switch, split tunneling, and double VPN for extra privacy. You can choose from 655 servers in 43 countries. Twenty-seven are in Canada, so you’ll have no trouble unblocking Canadian content or making a fast and secure connection from within Canada. The network runs at speeds between 1-10Gbps, depending on the individual server, and you can check the status on the website.

Mullvad is available on all your favorite platforms, including Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. However, it only supports five separate devices at once.

Surfshark
Best for Android

Spoof IP and GPS at the same time

Android smartphone users benefit from multiple servers in Canada and the ability to spoof your device's GPS to match your new VPN IP address. With both static and rotating IP features, you get full control over your location.

Pros
  • Android GPS masking
  • 3,200 servers with multiple in Canada
  • Blocks ads and tracking cookies
Cons
  • Free trial requires billing information
  • Windows app can be slow
$15.45/mo at Surfshark

Surfshark is a great all-rounded with 3,200 individual servers in more than 100 countries. This includes Montreal, Vancouver, and Toronto in Canada. It’s our pick for Android due to its unique GPS-overriding feature. This matches your GPS location to the VPN IP, ensuring you can unblock geo-restricted apps that require your phone’s location service.

Beyond Android, it supports iOS, Windows, macOS, and Linux. The easy-to-use app comes with a kill switch, advanced split tunneling (bypasser), and an ad blocker that prevents intrusive ads, popups, and tracking cookies. Our only gripe is that the Windows version can lag and requires more RAM than most.

Surfshark has an interesting approach to IP addresses. On one hand, you can use the IP rotation feature, which regularly changes your IP address within the same location. This makes it more difficult for your internet activity to be tracked. On the other hand, it provides free and premium static IPs, which keep your IP address the same for each session. This solves the problem of being labeled suspicious by websites or streaming services that view IP changes as a sign of account sharing or bot-like behavior.

A Surfshark VPN account starts at $15.45 a month, but you can get it for as low as $59.76 for two years ($2.39 per month). Technically, there is a 7-day free trial on mobile, but you must supply your billing information first and cancel in advance to avoid payment.

Private Internet Access
Largest number of servers

30,000 fast servers with four in Canada

PIA has the largest VPN server network available, speeds up to 10Gbps, and obfuscated technology. This prevents ISP throttling or website blocking in Canada or elsewhere.

Pros
  • Massive 10Gbps server network
  • Obfuscation technology
  • Blocks ads and malware
Cons
  • Based in the U.S.
  • iOS lacks split tunneling

Private Internet Access (PIA) is known for having the largest selection of individual servers of any leading VPN provider. In Canada, you can choose from options in Vancouver, Ontario, Toronto, and Montreal. Altogether, 30,000 are spread across 84 countries, allowing access to any geo-restricted content around the world.

It was most recently upgraded to a 10Gbps infrastructure, making it perfect for buffer-free streaming, fast file sharing, and low-ping gaming. What’s more, obfuscation completely hides the type of traffic, preventing ISPs from artificially reducing speed. Just get the relevant apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, or Android, and you can enjoy all the common VPN features. E.g., an automatic kill switch and split tunneling, though the latter is absent from the iOS version. You also get its proprietary MACE ad blocker, which uses DNS to block ads, web trackers, and malware.

PIA starts at $11.99 per month, $39.95 for a year ($3.33 per month), or $79 for three years ($2.03 per month). With no bandwidth or device limits, a single plan is suitable for large home networks and even the workplace. While you can pay in cryptocurrency, and it has a no-logs policy, the company is one of the few based in the U.S., which means it could still be ordered to hand over any data it might collect.

Final word on the best VPNs for Canada

In 2023, choosing the best VPN for Canada is not much different from anywhere else. You need fast servers, privacy, some leak-prevention features, and assurances that activities like streaming and gaming will go smoothly. For the average user, ExpressVPN ticks all these boxes and can unblock content in Canada and all over the world.

If you're having particular trouble with a Canadian ISP throttling traffic or blocking sites, NordVPN and TunnelBear's specialized servers are the better options. Likewise, CyberGhost and ProtonVPN excel at unblocking Canadian streaming services. Those on a budget can't go wrong with Mullvad's excellent flat monthly fee, while heavy Android users in Canada should consider Surfshark for its GPS masking feature.

ExpressVPN
Best overall

ExpressVPN boasts consistently fast speeds, impenetrable encryption, and multiple servers in 94 countries. Whether you're in Canada or want to access Canadian-only content, ExpressVPN's easy-to-use app gets things running in just a few clicks.

$12.95/mo at ExpressVPN
